title: Standpipe Operation — Engine Hose and Attack Hose

scene.description: Engine 181 is supplying a standpipe/FDC operation at a multi-story apartment building. A 100′ section of 2½″ hose runs from Engine 181 to the FDC. The standpipe carries water vertically to the fire floor. Use the elevation formula: height = (floor number - 1) × 10 ft, and elevation pressure = height × 0.5 PSI/ft. On the fire floor, a separate 2½″ attack hose is connected to the standpipe outlet and advanced to the nozzle.

sceneElements.elevation: Varies by problem. Elevation formula: height = (floor number - 1) × 10 ft; elevation pressure = height × 0.5 PSI/ft.

problems.title: Standpipe Operation — Engine Hose and Attack Hose
problems.question: Engine 181 is supplying the FDC with 100′ of 2½″ hose. The standpipe rises to Floor 3. Use the elevation formula: height = (floor number - 1) × 10 ft, and elevation pressure = height × 0.5 PSI/ft. A 150′ section of 2½″ attack hose is connected to the 3rd floor standpipe outlet and ends in a 1⅛″ smooth bore nozzle flowing 265 GPM at 50 PSI. Add 25 PSI for standpipe/FDC system loss. What pump discharge pressure should be set?
formulaBreakdown: Attack hose after standpipe = 150′ of 2½″ hose connected at the Floor 3 outlet
Nozzle after standpipe = 1⅛″ smooth bore nozzle flowing 265 GPM at 50 PSI NP
Elevation formula: height = (floor number - 1) × 10 ft; elevation pressure = height × 0.5 PSI/ft
PP = 35 FL + 50 NP + 10 elevation + 25 system loss
problems.instructorExplanation: Use the elevation formula: height = (floor number - 1) × 10 ft, and elevation pressure = height × 0.5 PSI/ft. For Floor 3, elevation is 20′ × 0.5 = 10 PSI. Then add the Engine-to-FDC hose friction loss, attack hose friction loss, nozzle pressure, and 25 PSI standpipe/FDC system loss.
problems.explainMistake: Common mistakes are treating the fire floor number as the number of floor rises, forgetting the Engine-to-FDC hose, only calculating the attack hose after the standpipe, forgetting the 25 PSI standpipe system loss, using the wrong C value for 2½″ hose, or missing the corrected elevation pressure for the fire floor.
